coredata.dc:title: Affinity-mediated photoluminescence quenching between metallic ions and surface functional groups of carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zhi-Liang
Xiao, Ru-Zhen
Jin, Jian-Cheng
Zeng, Li
Sun, Shuang-Jiao
Xu, Jia-Quan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) have been widely employed to detect metallic ions based on photoluminescence (PL) quenching. Nonetheless, the selectivity of detection is still dissatisfactory. This is due to the lack of sufficient knowledge on metallic ions quenching the PL of C-dots. Herein, affinity-mediated PL quenching between metallic ions with carboxyl- and hydroxyl-covered C-dots is revealed. The PL results showed that both Fe2+ and Fe3+ can quench the PL of C-dots, but the PL quenching kinetics are totally different. The carboxyl groups are demonstrated to preferentially coordinate with Fe3+ because of the high affinity between Fe3+ and carboxyl groups, while Fe2+ preferentially binds to hydroxyl groups. In addition, the PL quenching of two C-dots prepared by glucose and carbon fiber powder was further investigated after being treated with various metallic ions, confirming that the PL quenching depended on the affinity between metallic ions and the surface functional groups of C-dots. This study provided a better understanding of the PL quenching mechanism of C-dots by metallic ions and will contribute to the rational design of high specificity and selectivity C-dots-based sensors for discrimination of metallic ions in complex matrix samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Fe3+
Fe2+
Affinity-mediated
Photoluminescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL Quenching of C-dots by Fe3+ and Fe2+
Investigation of the PL quenching of functionalized C-dots by Fe2+ and Fe3+
Investigation of the PL quenching of different C-dots by metallic ions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Provincial (Nos. 2020JJ5515 , 2019JJ40272 ), and the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department of China (Nos. 19B519 , 20A454 ). The authors are grateful to Dr. Meng-Li Yang (Wuhan University) for their assistance of the C-dots synthesis.

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Provincial (Nos. 2020JJ5515, 2019JJ40272), and the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department of China (Nos.19B519, 20A454). The authors are grateful to Dr. Meng-Li Yang (Wuhan University) for their assistance of the C-dots synthesis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) corresponding size distribution of C-dots, scale bar is 20 nm, the inset is the HR-TEM image of C-dots. (c) FT-IR spectrum and (d) XPS spectrum of C-dots. The sizes are obtained by measuring 200 individual C-dot in TEM images.

PL spectra of C-dots treated with (a) Fe3+ (0 ~ 125 μM) and (b) Fe2+ (0 ~ 60 μM), with the inset representing plots of the PL intensity of C-dots against the Fe3+ or Fe2+. (c) Time course of the normalized PL intensity of C-dots (4.8 mg/10 mL) in the presence of Fe2+ (10 µM) or Fe3+ (10 µM). (d) Normalized PL intensity of C-dots treated with Fe3+ or Fe2+ (1 mM) before and after the addition of EDTA (5 mM), 1: C-dots, 2: C-dots + Fe3+, 3: C-dots + Fe2+, 4: C-dots + Fe3++EDTA, 5: C-dots+ Fe2++EDTA.
Characterization of functionalized C-dots. (a, b) FT-IR spectra, (c) 1 H NMR spectra and (d) XPS spectra of C-dots (red line), C-dots-PS (blue line), and C-dots-PS-NaOH (black line).
(a, b) Normalized PL intensity of three samples incubated with various concentrations of Fe3+ and Fe2+. (c) The schematic diagram for the binding of C-dots with Fe2+ and Fe3+.

(a) Relative PL intensity of C-dots, C-dots-PS and C-dots-PS-NaOH treated with Cu2+ and Ag+. ①: C-dots+Cu2+; ②: C-dots-PS+Cu2+; ③: C-dots-PS-NaOH+Cu2+; ④: C-dots+Ag+; ⑤: C-dots-PS+Ag+; ⑥: C-dots-PS-NaOH+Ag+. (b) TEM image of G-C-dots prepared with glucose. (c) FTIR spectra of G-C-dots and G-C-dots-PS prepared with glucose. (d) Relative PL intensity of G-C-dots and G-C-dots-PS treated with Cu2+, Ag+, Fe2+ and Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Affinity-mediated photoluminescence quenching between metallic ions and surface functional groups of carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shaoyang University, Shaoyang 422000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shaoyang University, Shaoyang, 422000, P. R China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Key Laboratory for Mass Spectrometry and Instrumentation, East China University of Technology, Nanchang 330013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Key Laboratory for Mass Spectrometry and Instrumentation, East China University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Key Laboratory for Mass Spectrometry and Instrumentation, East China University of Technology, Nanchang, 330013, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) have been widely employed to detect metallic ions based on photoluminescence (PL) quenching. Nonetheless, the selectivity of detection is still dissatisfactory. This is due to the lack of sufficient knowledge on metallic ions quenching the PL of C-dots. Herein, affinity-mediated PL quenching between metallic ions with carboxyl- and hydroxyl-covered C-dots is revealed. The PL results showed that both Fe2+ and Fe3+ can quench the PL of C-dots, but the PL quenching kinetics are totally different. The carboxyl groups are demonstrated to preferentially coordinate with Fe3+ because of the high affinity between Fe3+ and carboxyl groups, while Fe2+ preferentially binds to hydroxyl groups. In addition, the PL quenching of two C-dots prepared by glucose and carbon fiber powder was further investigated after being treated with various metallic ions, confirming that the PL quenching depended on the affinity between metallic ions and the surface functional groups of C-dots. This study provided a better understanding of the PL quenching mechanism of C-dots by metallic ions and will contribute to the rational design of high specificity and selectivity C-dots-based sensors for discrimination of metallic ions in complex matrix samples.

Through selective protection of the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of C-dots, we reveal that Fe3+ and Cu2+ is coordinated with the carboxyl groups of C-dots, while Fe2+ and Ag+ preferentially combines with hydroxyl groups, confirming that the affinity between metallic ions and functional groups of C-dots plays an important role in the PL quenching process.

ce:section.ce:para: Metallic ions widely exist and play an important role in environmental and physiological processes. Developing an excellent analysis method for distinguishing metallic ions is significant and constantly attracts extensive attention. To date, a variety of strategies have been reported to detect metallic ions, such as electrochemical methods, atomic absorption spectroscopy, and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [1–3]. Although the accuracy of the above-mentioned strategies is excellent, specialized instruments and sophisticated assay procedures are required, which impedes their further application. Thus, the development of novel methods is highly desirable. Over the past two decades, fluorescent techniques have attracted increasing attention for sensing metallic ions. A larger number of fluorescent nanoprobes have been represented for sensitive and selective detection of metallic ions in the field of environmental monitoring [4] and biomedical imaging [5].

Carbon nanodots (C-dots), a fantastic carbon nanomaterial with sizes below 10 nm and covered with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups [6–8], have been utilized to detect metallic ions (e.g., Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, etc.) because of high sensitivity and feasibility [9–14]. Zhou et al. [15] reported that the photoluminescence (PL) of C-dots can be quenched by adding Hg2+ and can also be recovered by removing Hg2+ with biothiols because of their stronger affinity; thus, unfunctionalized C-dots can be utilized as fluorescence sensors for the detection of Hg2+ with a detection limit of 4.2 nM. Wei et al. [16] reported that C-dots can be employed as fluorescent probes for “off-on” detection of Cu2+ and L-cysteine in aqueous solution, the detection limit is 2.3 × 10−8 M for Cu2+, and 3.4 × 10−10 M for L-Cys. Chen et al. [13] reported that B, N, S-co-Doped C-dot was prepared and used for colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing Fe3+, the detection limit as low as 90 nM can be achieved. Unfortunately, C-dots prepared with different strategies usually produce different PL quenching results by the same metallic ions. For example, Huang et al. [17] reported that Cu2+ can selectively quench the PL of C-dots prepared by carbonizing polyethylenimine. However, Liu et al. [10] reported that Ni2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ag+ and Mn2+ can quench the PL of C-dots prepared by carbon fiber powder refluxed in a nitric acid solution. This is due to insufficient knowledge on the key factors of metallic ions quenching the PL of C-dots. Therefore, it is imperative that we reveal the key factors of metallic ions affecting the PL of C-dots for the rational design of C-dots-based sensors for the discrimination of metallic ions.

Herein, to better understand the key factors, C-dots covered with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups were prepared by refluxing carbon fiber powder in a nitric acid solution and were used to analyze Fe2+ and Fe3+, which widely exist in organisms and the environment. The results show that both Fe2+ and Fe3+ can quench the PL of C-dots, but their quenching kinetics curves are totally different. Furthermore, through selective protection of the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of C-dots, we reveal that Fe3+ is coordinated with the carboxyl groups of C-dots, while Fe2+ preferentially combines with hydroxyl groups, resulting in different quenching kinetics. In addition, the PL quenching of two kinds of C-dots synthesized by different strategies was further investigated after adding various metallic ions (Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Mg2+ and Al3+), confirming that the affinity between metallic ions and functional groups of C-dots plays an important role in the PL quenching process. Finally, based on our results and peer researches, we made a conclusion that the PL quenching between C-dots and metallic ions involved two steps: binding and quenching. This study is helpful to understand the key factors of metallic ions affecting the PL of C-dots, as well as to the rational design of C-dots-based sensors for the discrimination of metallic ions in complex matrices.

In a typical synthesis, approximately 0.5 g of carbon fiber powder (Shanghai Tansu Manufactory) dispersed in 100 mL of nitric acid solution (10 M) was heated to 120 ℃ and refluxed for 4 h. The resulting C-dot solution was treated with sodium hydroxide(NaOH, 1 M) to pH 3, and the NaOH-treated C-dot solution was filtered by BIOSHARP membrane filters (0.22 µm) and dialyzed by a 3500-Da dialysis bag for 5 days. Finally, the dialyzed C-dot solution was concentrated by Millipore centrifugal filter devices at a speed of 6000 rpm. Accordingly, the effective fractions equivalent to < 3 kDa were collected and dried by a vacuum freeze-drying machine.

Briefly, glucose (20 g) and oil acid (30 mL) were heated at 215 ℃ for 8 min. The resulting mixture was then cooled and maintained at 180 ℃ for 30 min, and a brownish solid sample (G-C-dots) was obtained. After removing the heating source, the G-C-dots samples were cooled to room temperature. After centrifugation, the precipitated G-C-dots samples were dissolved in ultrapure water and extracted with hexane to remove residual oleic acid. Finally, the G-C-dots samples were dialyzed by a 5000-Da dialysis bag for 5 days and dried by a vacuum freeze-drying machine.

The C-dots were functionalized by PS and hydrolyzed by NaOH to selectively protect the surface functional groups. Briefly, approximately 10 mg of C-dots dispersed in ultrapure water (2 mL) was added to 20 mL of 1,4-dioxane containing 2 g of 1,3-propane sultone (PS) in a flask. Then, approximately 2 mL of triethylamine (TEA) was added dropwise to the flask. After stirring for 24 h at room temperature, the solvent was removed under vacuum. The resulting samples dispersed in ultrapure water were then dialyzed in sodium chloride (NaCl, 0.1 M) solution for 1 day to remove the residual TEA. Finally, the PS-reacted C-dots (C-dots-PS) were dialyzed by a 3500-Da dialysis bag with ultrapure water for 3 days and dried in a vacuum freeze-drying machine.

Approximately 10 mg of C-dots-PS was added to 30 mL of NaOH solution (0.5 mol/L). After stirring for 24 h at room temperature, the NaOH-treated C-dots-PS solution (C-dots-PS-NaOH) was neutralized with hydrochloric acid (1 M), dialyzed by a 3500-Da dialysis bag for 3 days and further dried in a vacuum freeze-drying machine. The functionalization of G-C-dots was carried out by the procedure of C-dots.

The C-dots were prepared following a previous report [8]. The carbon fiber powder was exfoliated with a nitric acid solution (10 M), followed by neutralization with NaOH and dialysis. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results in Fig. 1a showed images of the prepared nearly spherical C-dots. Furthermore, a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1a, inset) showed that the lattice spacing of a typical C-dot was ~3.2 Å. The corresponding statistical diameter of the C-dots was 3.2 ± 1.1 nm (Fig. 1b), displaying an extremely small diameter. Under excitation at 400 nm, the C-dots emit green fluorescence with a maximum emission peak centered at 535 nm (Fig. S1), and the corresponding quantum yield is ~7%, which was calculated by a commercialized system (details in the SI).

Next, the surface functional groups of C-dots were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1c, based on the four contributions fitted from the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of C-dots, the binding energies of 284.5, 286.2, 287.8, and 289.0 eV can be attributed to CC, C−O, CO, and O−CO, respectively, which is in agreement with previous reports [8,18,19]. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum of C-dots displayed four characteristic peaks (Fig. 1d), which can be assigned to hydroxyl (O−H, ~3400 cm−1), carboxyl (CO, ~1725 cm−1), carbonyl (CO, ~1605 cm−1), and C−O (~1240 cm−1) groups [8,18,19]. Based on these observations, we confirm that the C-dot surface is covered with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, including hydroxyl (O−H), carboxyl (−COOH), and carbonyl (CO) groups. The oxygen-containing functional groups covered on C-dots are beneficial to water solubilization, as well as to forming complexes with metallic ions, quenching the PL of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL Quenching of C-dots by Fe3+ and Fe2+
ce:section.ce:para: The PL spectra of C-dots were recorded after being treated with various concentrations of Fe3+ and Fe2+ for 5 s [20]. As displayed in Fig. 2a, the PL intensity decreased gradually as Fe3+ increased in the range of 0 − 125 μM, and the corresponding Stern-Volmer quenching constant (KSV) was 6.2 × 103 M−1 (Fig. 2a, inset). Interestingly, a new PL emission peak below 450 nm appears, and we speculate that this may be the PL of FeCl3 (Fig. S2). As the Fe2+ concentration increased from 0 to 60 μM, the PL intensity also decreased obviously (Fig. 2b), and the corresponding KSV was 2.5 × 103 M−1 (Fig. 2b, inset). These results indicated that the PL quenching of C-dots with Fe3+ was better than that with Fe2+. As shown in Fig. S3, three samples, including C-dots, Fe2+-treated C-dots and Fe3+-treated C-dots, showed identical absorption spectra profiles, suggesting that the structures of C-dots are maintained after incubation with Fe3+ and Fe2+. Furthermore, using the time-resolved photoluminescence technique, the fluorescence lifetime was fitted to 3.7 ns for C-dots, 2.9 ns for Fe3+-treated C-dots, and 3.3 ns for Fe2+-treated C-dots (Fig. S4), indicating that the PL quenching of C-dots with Fe3+ is more sensitive than that with Fe2+. Additionally, the PL quenching kinetics of C-dots were recorded (Fig. 2c), and we noticed that the PL intensity of C-dots-incubated Fe3+ (10 µM) was quenched by approximately 15% in 5 min and slowly decreased by approximately 23% in the next 55 min (Fig. 2c, green line). In contrast, the PL intensity of C-dots-incubated Fe2+ (10 µM) was quenched by approximately 5% in an hour (Fig. 2c, red line). In a pioneering work, Yan reported the different PL quenching kinetics of Fe3+- and Fe2+-treated GSH-CdTe QDs [21], and the authors suggested that this different quenching behavior probably originated from various electronic structures and redox potentials of the quencher [21].

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA), a typical chelating agent that can form stable chelates with various metallic ions, was utilized to further explore the PL quenching of C-dots with Fe2+ and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S5, the PL intensity of C-dots remained after adding EDTA (5 mM). In contrast, both Fe3+ and Fe2+ can obviously decrease the PL of C-dots. After being treated with EDTA, the PL intensity of Fe3+ and Fe2+-treated C-dots can be recovered again (Fig. 2d), suggesting that the PL quenching of C-dots with both Fe3+ and Fe2+ is reversible. Based on these results, we speculate that some definite functional groups on C-dots coordinate with Fe2+ and Fe3+. When incubated with EDTA, Fe3+ and Fe2+ can escape from the surface functional groups of C-dots and form a stable chelate with EDTA, so the PL intensity of C-dots is recovered again.

In general, two prerequisites are required for the PL quenching of C-dots. One is the quencher (e.g., metallic ions, anions and biomolecules), and the electronic structures and redox potentials of the quencher dominate the quenching capacity [21–24]. The other is closer distance interaction, such as the coordination of metallic ions on C-dots, which is dependent on the concentration and affinity. Based on the abovementioned experimental results, we speculate that the affinity between the surface functional groups of C-dots and the different species of iron (Fe3+ and Fe2+) should also be responsible for the different quenching kinetics curves, except for the electronic structures and redox potentials of the quencher [21]. In a pioneering work, Zhang et al. demonstrated that phenolic hydroxyl groups coordinate with Fe3+ and quench the fluorescence of graphitic carbon quantum dots [20]. In addition, Liu et al. reported that Cu2+ preferentially combines with the carboxyl groups of C-dots, quenching the fluorescence of C-dots [8]. For the as-prepared C-dots in this work, which were covered with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on C-dots, some definite functional groups should coordinate with Fe3+ or Fe2+ and quench the PL of C-dots. Herein, to eliminate the interference caused by electronic structure and oxidation-reduction, we focused on exploring the effects of affinity between the surface functional groups of C-dots and metallic ions through selective protection and deprotection of the surface functional groups of C-dots.

1,3-Propane sultone (PS) was employed to selectively protect the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on C-dots according to previous literature with a minor change [25,26]. As displayed in Fig. S6, the hydroxyl group can be converted to ether, and the carboxyl groups can be converted to an ester that can be further hydrolyzed under alkaline conditions to form carboxyl groups, while ethers cannot. Therefore, the selective protection of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of C-dots can be achieved. The PS-grafted C-dots were abbreviated as C-dots-PS, and the NaOH-treated C-dots-PS was abbreviated as C-dots-PS-NaOH.

Then, the FT-IR spectra of three kinds of samples were collected. Compared to C-dots, C-dots-PS showed a series of new absorption peaks at 611, 528, 1195, and 1046 cm−1 ( Fig. 3a, blue line). According to a previous report, these peaks can be attributed to the characteristic absorption of sulfonic acid [8], suggesting that the PS molecules were grafted onto C-dots successfully. After C-dots-PS was treated with NaOH, the relative intensity of the sulfonic acid (at 611, 528, 1195, and 1046 cm−1) and carboxyl groups (at 1725 cm−1) decreased (Fig. 3a, black line). However, compared to C-dots, the characteristic peak of sulfonic acid was still obvious in C-dots-PS-NaOH, which can be attributed to the PS residues of ethers, implying that there are more hydroxyl than carboxyl groups on C-dots. Interestingly, the stretching vibration frequency of CO on C-dots-PS redshifted from 1725 to 1735 cm−1 compared with C-dots (Fig. 3b, blue line). After hydrolysis with NaOH, the stretching vibration of CO on C-dots-PS-NaOH returned to 1725 cm−1 (Fig. 3b, black line), suggesting that the ester groups were hydrolyzed sufficiently and that the protection of hydroxyl groups was achieved for C-dots-PS-NaOH.

Compared to the C-dots, C-dots-PS displayed a series of new absorption peaks at 2.11, 2.90, and 4.48 ppm in the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 3c, blue line), which can be attributed to β-, α-, and γ-H of sulfonic acid (Fig. S6), respectively, indicating that synchronous protection of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups was achieved for C-dots-PS [8]. After being treated with NaOH, the chemical shift of γ-H was 4.42 ppm for C-dots-PS-NaOH and lower than that of C-dots-PS (Fig. 3c, black line) because the PS residues of ether cannot be hydrolyzed by NaOH [8]. Therefore, we further confirmed that the hydroxyl groups were protected for C-dots-PS-NaOH, which is in agreement with the FT-IR results.

Then, three samples were investigated by XPS. Compared to C-dots, the ratio of sulfur-to-carbon increased from 0 to 8.2 and 6.5 for C-dots-PS (Fig. 3d, blue line) and C-dots-PS-NaOH, respectively (Fig. 3 d, black line), which is in agreement with the FT-IR and 1H NMR results that the hydroxyl groups were protected selectively for C-dots-PS-NaOH. The C-dots-PS-NaOH (Fig. 3d, black line) and C-dots-PS (Fig. 3d, blue line) show a relatively strong nitrogen peak with the binding energy of ~400 eV compared with the C-dots (Fig. 3d, red line), We speculate that this nitrogen peak can attribute to the residual TEA. However, the residual amino groups would not affect the binding studies because a comparative study of quenching efficiency will carry out for C-dots-PS-NaOH and C-dots-PS both of which had the same amino groups. In addition, the XPS results further suggested that the number of hydroxyls was greater than that of carboxyl groups on C-dots. Based on the above results, we assert that the protection of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups is achieved simultaneously for C-dots-PS, while only the hydroxyl groups are protected for C-dots-PS-NaOH. By comparing the quenching efficiency of Fe3+ and Fe2+ on three kinds of samples, the binding capacity of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups with Fe3+ and Fe2+ can be distinguished.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the PL quenching of functionalized C-dots by Fe2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The PL spectra of three kinds of samples were recorded after adding Fe3+ and Fe2+ for 5 s [24]. As shown in Fig. 4a, after adding Fe3+ (0.1 mM), the PL intensity of C-dots (black line) and C-dots-PS-NaOH (blue line) was reduced by more than ~40%, while ~87% of the PL intensity was retained for C-dots-PS (red line), suggesting that Fe3+ was preferentially coordinated with the carboxyl groups. As shown in Fig. 4b, the PL intensity of C-dots-PS-NaOH (blue line) and C-dots-PS (red line) decreased slightly with the addition of Fe2+ (0.1 mM), while the PL intensity of C-dots (black line) was reduced by ~32%. Because the hydroxyl groups have been protected for C-dots-PS and C-dots-PS-NaOH, Fe2+ was considered to preferentially bind with hydroxyl groups, quenching the PL of C-dots. Although C-dots are regarded to be covered with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, they are mainly hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [6,8,27,28]. For the as-prepared C-dots of this work, the number of hydroxyls is greater than that of carboxyl groups on C-dots; thus, we speculate that the affinity of carboxyl groups with Fe3+ is stronger than the affinity of carboxyl groups with Fe2+. Based on the abovementioned results, a diagram of Fe3+ and Fe2+ coordinating with the surface functional groups of C-dots is proposed. As shown in Fig. 4c, Fe3+ preferentially binds with the carboxyl groups of C-dots, while Fe2+ preferentially coordinates with hydroxyl groups, quenching the PL of C-dots. We speculate that this mainly originates from their different association constants. To demonstrate this point, the complexation of Fe3+ and Fe2+ with salicylic acid, benzoic acid and phenol was investigated by mass spectrometry (Fig. S7–9). As shown in Fig. S7, a chelate between Fe3+ and salicylic acid was detected by mass spectrometry in the form of [Fe(C7H5O3)2]+, which was consistent with the results displayed in Fig. 4a that Fe3+ can quench the PL of C-dots. As shown in Fig. S8, a chelate between Fe3+ and benzoic acid was also detected by mass spectrometry in the form of [Fe(C7H5O2)2(C7H6O2)]+, which was also consistent with the results displayed in Fig. 4a that Fe3+ can quench the PL of C-dots-PS+NaOH. Besides, compared with Fig. S7 and Fig. S8, the signal intensity of [Fe(C7H5O2)2(C7H6O2)]+ (7.98E4) was lower than [Fe(C7H5O3)2]+ (4.52E5), indicating that the association constant of [Fe(C7H5O2)2(C7H6O2)]+ was lower than [Fe(C7H5O3)2]+ [29], which was also agreement with the results displayed in Fig. 4a that the quenching efficiency of C-dots-PS+NaOH was lower than C-dots. In Fig. S9, a chelate between Fe2+ and phenol was detected by mass spectrometry in the form of [Fe(C6H5O)]+ and no chelate was detected between Fe2+ and benzoic acid, which was consistent with the results displayed in Fig. 4b that Fe2+ can quench the PL of C-dots and cannot quench the PL of C-dots-PS+NaOH. Furthermore, compared with Fig. S7–9, the signal intensity of [Fe(C6H5O)]+ (1.15E4) was the lowest, which was also consistent with the results that lowest quenching efficiency of Fe2+-C-dots was lower than Fe3+- C-dots-PS+NaOH and Fe3+-C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the PL quenching of different C-dots by metallic ions
ce:section.ce:para: To further confirm the significance of affinity for PL quenching of C-dots with metallic ions. The PL intensity of C-dots synthesized with different methods treated with various metallic ions was also assessed. We first examined the PL quenching results of C-dots, C-dots-PS and C-dots-PS-NaOH by different metallic ions. As shown in Figs. S10–15, no tremendous PL quenching of C-dots was observed after treatment with Al3+ (Figs. S10 and S11), Mg2+ (Figs. S12 and S13) and Zn2+ (Figs. S14 and S15), indicating a negligible influence of these three kinds of metal ions on our sensing system, agreeing with a pioneering work [30]. In contrast, the addition of Cu2+ and Ag+ led to an obvious decrease in the PL intensity ( Fig. 5a). After treatment with Cu2+, the PL intensity decreased 90% for C-dots (Fig. 5a, sample ①, Fig. S16), ~42% for C-dots-PS (Fig. 5a, sample ②, Fig. S17) and ~80% for C-dots-PS-NaOH (Fig. 5a, sample ③, Fig. S18), which can be attributed to the higher affinity and faster chelating rate between carboxyl groups and Cu2+ than between hydroxyl groups and Cu2+, agreeing with the Sun’s results that Cu2+ decreases the PL of C-dots strongly than that of Ag+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, etc [31]. After incubation with Ag+, the PL intensity was reduced by more than ~50% for C-dots (Fig. 5a, sample ④, Fig. S19), ~30% for C-dots-PS (Fig. 5a, sample ⑤, Fig. S20), and ~32% for C-dots-PS-NaOH (Fig. 5a, sample ⑥, Fig. S21), indicating that the binding ability of Ag+ to hydroxyl groups is stronger than that to carboxyl groups.

Additionally, new C-dots were first prepared by refluxing glucose and oleic acid at 210 ℃, followed by extraction with hexane and dialysis with ultrapure water [30,32]. The new C-dots prepared with glucose are abbreviated as G-C-dots. As shown in Fig. 5b, G-C-dots are spherical and have good dispersity, the corresponding statistic diameters of G-C-dots are 3.6 ± 0.9 nm (Fig. S22), and they emit a green florescence with a maximum emission peak position at 505 nm (Fig. S23). Next, the surface functional groups of G-C-dots were assessed. The FT-IR spectrum of G-C-dots displayed two strong characteristic absorption bands (Fig. 5c, black line), which can be assigned to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups (O−H, ~3400 cm−1) and the stretching vibration of C−O (~1032 cm−1), suggesting that the G-C-dots were covered with abundant hydroxyl groups (O−H) [29,31]. After functionalization of G-C-dots with PS, the resulting samples were abbreviated as G-C-dots-PS. Compared with G-C-dots (Fig. 5c, black line), two new absorption peaks at 1048 and 1172 cm−1 appeared (Fig. 5c, red line), which can be attributed to the characteristic absorption of sulfonic acid. In addition, the stretching vibration frequency of partial C-H shifted from 2928 cm−1 to 2974 cm−1, which was due to the grafting of sulfonic acid. All of these results indicated that the hydroxyl group of G-C-dots was converted to ether, and G-C-dots-PS was prepared successfully.

We also determined the PL quenching efficiency of Cu2+, Ag+, Fe3+ and Fe2+ on G-C-dots and G-C-dots-PS. As shown in Fig. 5d, Cu2+ and Fe3+ led to the obvious PL quenching of C-dots, which is consistent with previous reports [8,14]. In the presence of Cu2+, the PL intensity of G-C-dots was similar to that of G-C-dots-PS (Figs. S24 and S25), indicating the relatively weak binding affinity of hydroxyl groups with Cu2+. After adding Ag+ (Figs. S26 and S27) and Fe2+ (Figs. S28 and S29), the PL intensity of G-C-dots-PS increased compared with that of G-C-dots, implying the good binding ability of hydroxyl groups with Ag+ and Fe2+. In addition, the PL intensity of Fe3+-treated G-C-dots-PS was stronger than that of Fe3+-treated G-C-dots (Figs. S30 and S31), indicating that hydroxyl groups have good affinity for Fe3+, similar to carboxyl groups. Significantly, the PL intensity of G-dots-PS treated with Ag+ or Fe2+ can be obviously recovered compared with that treated with Fe3+ and Cu2+, ascribed to the stronger affinity of hydroxyl groups with Ag+ and Fe2+, agreeing with the results of Fig. 4b. Based on the abovementioned results, we assert that the affinity mediates PL quenching between metallic ions and the surface functional groups of C-dots. In addition, we speculate that whether the energy of excited C-dots can be transferred to metallic ions is of great significance for PL quenching, that is, the matching degree between C-dots and metallic ions. For example, the matching of the size of C-dots with the electronic structure of metallic ions. Unfortunately, although we have revealed the key factors of metallic ions quenching the PL of C-dots, the selective detection of targeted metallic ions in actual samples is still a challenge. Further efforts focusing on the quantitative analysis of the surface functional groups of C-dots and their association constants with metallic ions and further investigation of the PL quenching mechanism of C-dots with metallic ions will be helpful for the progress of C-dots-based sensors for the selective detection of targeted metallic ions in complex matrix samples.

In summary, the PL quenching between carboxyl- and hydroxyl-covered C-dots and various metallic ions (Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Mg2+ and Al3+) was systematically investigated. Combined with our results and previous studies, it can be concluded that PL quenching between C-dots and metallic ions involves two steps, binding and quenching. The binding step provided a close interaction distance, which depended on the association constants between C-dots and metallic ions. The quenching step is the energy transfer from the excited C-dots to the metallic ions, which depends on the electronic structures and redox potentials of the metallic ions. This work presents a significant study on the key factors of metallic ions affecting the PL of C-dots, which will guide the rational design of surface functionalized C-dots-based sensors for the discrimination of metallic ions in complex matrix samples.

Zhi-Liang Chen and Jia-Quan Xu: designed and performed the experimental section, and wrote this manuscript. Ru-Zhen Xiao, Jian-cheng Jin, Li Zeng and Shuang-Jiao Sun: analysis and discussion the experimental results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest and no conflicts of interest.

The authors are grateful to Dr. Meng-Li Yang (Wuhan University) for their assistance of the C-dots synthesis.
